blocks.content: Daisy's study group met on Monday and Wednesday evenings in the library — four people from her American literature seminar, working through papers together. Three of them treated it like work. The fourth, Jordan Tate, treated it like an extended conversation he happened to be having with Daisy.

blocks.speaker: Jordan
blocks.content: You read faster than anyone I've ever met.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I've always read fast.

blocks.speaker: Jordan
blocks.content: You also annotate in the margins. In pen.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Is that a problem.

blocks.speaker: Jordan
blocks.content: No, it's — I like it. You commit to your opinions.
He was pre-law from Chicago. Sharp, well-read, confident. Daisy noticed.
After study group one Wednesday, walking out into the cold:

blocks.speaker: Jordan
blocks.content: There's a coffee place on Washington that's open until midnight. Good for getting out of the library.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I've seen it.

blocks.speaker: Jordan
blocks.content: We could go sometime. Just to work somewhere different.
She looked at him. He was being careful about it — framing it as the coffee, the change of scenery.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I appreciate that. I can't though.

blocks.speaker: Jordan
blocks.content: Someone back home.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Something like that.

blocks.speaker: Jordan
blocks.content: Something like that.
He let it go. They walked back toward the dorms in opposite directions. She pulled out her phone.
you awake
always. what's up
nothing. just wanted to say hey
hey
hey
